rulings.Case Title: TOKIO MARINE SPECIALTY INS. CO VS. LYFT, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner Tokio Marine Specialty Ins. Co Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Vacate Arbitration Award; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities ***Redacted*** (Added For Tentative Ruling Entry Purposes Only)
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Wednesday, August 8, 2018, Line 1, Petitioner Tokio Marine Specialty Ins. Co.'s Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Vacate Arbitration Award; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities ***Redacted*** (Added For Tentative Ruling Entry Purposes Only) (Part 2 of 2) If Tokio's claimed exception applied to Tokio's employee/auto exclusion argument, an arbitration award would need to be vacated every time an arbitrator failed to resolve all of the arguments made by the losing party even when the arbitrator, based on her other rulings, determined that the unresolved arguments would have no effect on who prevails in the arbitration or the relief granted in the award. This is not the law. Nothing in the California Arbitration Act or any post-Moncharsh cases construing that Act remotely supports Tokio's position and it is clearly contrary to the well-settled principles of limited judicial review of arbitration awards. An arbitrator, every bit as much as a judge, is free to decide which arguments made by the parties she believes are material to the decision she needs to make and, unlike a judge, if her decision not to address and resolve a certain argument is based on her legal or factual determination that the argument is immaterial to the decision she needs to make, the decision not to address the argument is immune from judicial scrutiny based on the rule of no review for legal or factual error. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Lyft is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: TOKIO MARINE SPECIALTY INS. CO VS. LYFT, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Vacate Arbitration Award; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities ***Redacted***
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Wednesday, August 8, 2018, Line 1, PETITIONER TOKIO MARINE SPECIALTY INS. CO.'S Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Vacate Arbitration Award; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities ***Redacted*** (Part 1 of 2) Petitioner Tokio Marine Specialty Insurance Company's petition to vacate the arbitration award is denied. The critical issue on this petition is not whether the arbitrators (totally) failed to address and resolve Tokio's argument that the auto exclusion applied and thus there was no coverage under the parties' policy for the injuries assertedly suffered by Mr. Kim because Mr. Asresse was a Lyft employee, but why the arbitrators chose not to address and resolve Tokio's argument. The arbitrators did not decide Tokio's "employee/auto exclusion argument" because, in the view of the majority of the arbitrators, regardless of how that argument was resolved, Tokio would not be able to recoup from Lyft all or any part of the $1 million it paid to Mr. Kim. Although not stated precisely this way in the arbitration award, two of the arbitrators determined that Mr. Kim's claim that Lyft negligently hired, supervised and retained Mr. Asresse and negligently entrusted him to drive was a covered claim even if Mr. Asresse was a Lyft employee and the auto exclusion applied. Again without stating so precisely, the two arbitrators determined that, with regard to Mr. Kim's claim of negligent, hiring, supervision, retention and entrustment, the parties' policy provided coverage for that claim and such coverage was not eliminated or adversely affected by the auto exclusion even if that exclusion was applicable because Mr. Asresse was a Lyft employee. These determinations of the two arbitrators - the reason why they chose not to address and resolve Tokio's employee/auto exclusion argument -- might be a misinterpretation of the parties' insurance policy and/or contrary to insurance law, an issue that this court does not reach. However, even if these determinations were erroneous, they are unreviewable legal errors. Per well-settled California law, absent an agreement of the parties to the contrary, a court is not empowered to vacate an arbitration award because of one or more legal errors made by an arbitrator. (Moncharsh v. Heily & Blase (1992) 3 Cal 4th 1, 6 ("an arbitrator's decision is not generally reviewable for errors of fact or law, whether or not such error appears on the face of the award and causes substantial injustice to the parties.")). Tokio's effort to fit within a claimed exception to the rule of no review for legal errors when the "record shows that an issue has been submitted to the arbitrator and he totally failed to consider it" based on a case decided prior to Moncharsh is unavailing. Even assuming that such an exception applies to a situation where the arbitrators expressly acknowledges that they are not deciding an issue and such an exception survives Moncharsh because it is a correct interpretation of CCP 1286.2(a)(5), both of which are doubtful, that exception has not application to this case. Where, as here, arbitrators choose not to address and resolve an issue based on their determination that their rulings on other legal and factual issues made it unnecessary to do so, the claimed error of the arbitrators is not within the exception to the rule of no review for legal error asserted by Tokio. (Tentative Ruling Continued in Part 2 of 2) =(302/HEK)

rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Wednesday, August 8, 2018, Line 1, RESPONDENT LYFT, INC.'S Notice Of Hearing On Cross-Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award. Respondent Lyft, Inc.'s cross-petition to confirm arbitration award is granted. The arbitration award entitled "Final Award" in JAMS Arbitration Case Reference No. 1100085960 dated May 3. 2018. Because the court has rejected the petition of petitioner Tokio Marine Specialty Insurance Company to vacate the arbitration award and there are no other grounds that have been identified by Tokio that the award should not be confirmed, Lyft is entitled to have the award confirmed. rulings.Case Title: TOKIO MARINE SPECIALTY INS. CO VS. LYFT, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To File And Keep Confidential Materials Under Seal
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Wednesday, August 8, 2018, Line 1, RESPONDENT LYFT, INC.'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To File And Keep Confidential Materials Under Seal. Respondent Lyft, Inc.'s motion to file and keep confidential materials under seal is denied. While in some circumstances a private agreement to keep materials confidential could constitute an overriding interest for purposes of CRC 2.550 and assuming without deciding that the Lyft-Tokio agreement does constitute an overriding interest (but see CRC 2.551(a) ("The court must not permit a record to be filed under seal based solely on the agreement or stipulation of the parties.")), Lyft has not shown that this overriding interest will be prejudiced if the documents it seeks to have sealed are made available to the public. Due to the important constitutional principles at stake in open proceedings in all civil cases, the proponent of a sealing order must make a "specific showing of serious injury" that it would suffer if the documents are made available to the public. (Universal City Studios, Inc. v. Superior Court (2003) 100 Cal. App. 4th 1273, 1282). "In delineating the injury to be prevented, specificity is essential. Broad allegations of harm, bereft of specific examples or articulated reasoning, are insufficient." (Id. quoting In re Cendent Corp. (3rd Cir. 2001) 260 F.3d 183, 194). The only injury that Lyft has identified that it or Tokio would suffer if the documents filed in this case are made public is the parties "will lose the agreed-upon expectation that the matters arising from their coverage dispute will remain confidential." Case law construing rule 2.550 makes clear that this claimed injury falls far short of what is required for a sealing order. (See, e.g., McNair v. National Collegiate Athletic Association (2015) 234 Cal. App. 4th 25; Huffy Corp. v. Superior Court (2003) 112 Cal. A00. 4th 97; Universal City Studios, supra). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Lyft is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: PETER MAKRAS ET AL VS. UCSF MEDICAL CENTER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Have Requests For Admissions Set Two To Peter Makras Deemed Admitted With Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 8, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANT ERIC HANSEN, BRENDA STENGELE, THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA's Motion To Have Requests For Admissions Set Two To Peter Makras Deemed Admitted With Request For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing Required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARK MAKOWIECKI VS. PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC COMPANY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Wednesday, August 8, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC COMPANY, PG&E CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants PG&E Corporation and Pacific Gas & Electric Company, Inc.'s demurrer to all eight causes of action in the second amended complaint filed by plaintiff Mark Makowiecki is: sustained without leave to amend as to the first cause of action for breach of the implied contract of continued employment and is overruled as to the remaining causes of action. Mr. Makowiecki has not opposed PG&E's demurrer to the first cause of action. As to the second cause of action for slander per se, Mr. Makowiecki has sufficiently alleged the substance of the slanderous statements and has alleged that they are false. Mr. Makowiecki has also alleged at least one slanderous statement that is not time-barred by the slander limitations period. As to the third and fourth causes of action for intentional and negligent interference with contract and /or prospective economic relationships, Mr. Makowiecki has sufficiently alleged the slanderous statements as independently wrongful conduct, although no independently wrongful conduct needs to be alleged for the interference with contract claims. As to the fifth and eighth causes of action for violation of the Unfair Competition Law and injunctive relief, the facts alleged to support the slander and interference claims also support the UCL claim and serve as a basis for injunctive relief. As to the sixth and seventh causes of action for intentional and negligent infliction of emotional distress, Mr. Makowiecki has alleged extreme and outrageous conduct by PG&E by attempting to prevent Mr. Makowiecki from being employable in his chosen field. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Makowiecki is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)
